case_title: ALBERT TINDLE VS. UNITED PARCEL SERVICE OF AMERICA, INC. A DELAWARE ET AL

docket_entries.description: AMENDMENT TO COMPLAINT PER ORDER DEFENDANT SUED INCORRECTLY AS UNITED PARCEL SERVICE OF AMERICA, INC. A DELAWARE TO BE UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC.

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC'S DEMURRER TO ALL FOUR CAUSES OF ACTION ALLEGED IN THE COMPLAINT FILED BY ALBERT TINDLE IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION FOR NEGLIGENT HIRING AND SUSTAINED WITH TWENTY DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE SECOND, THIRD, AND FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR ASSAULT, BATTERY, INTENTIONAL INFLICTION OF EMOTIONAL DISTRESS (SEE WORD MINUTESS FOR DETAILS) ORDER SIGNED. calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-30-2018 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-30-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendant United Parcel Service, Inc's demurrer to all four causes of action alleged in the complaint filed by Albert Tindle is sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for negligent hiring and sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the second, third, and fourth causes of action for assault, battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress. "Where the complaint affirmatively alleges facts indicating coverage by the workers' compensation laws, if it fails to state additional facts negating application of the exclusive remedy provision, no civil action will lie and the complaint is subject to a general demurrer." (Roberts v. Pup 'N' Taco Driveup (1984) 160 Cal.App.3d 278, 284). The negligent hiring claim is barred by the exclusivity rule and there is no reasonable possibility that Mr. Tindle can state a viable negligent hiring claim. Claims against an employer for negligent hiring predicated on an assault by a negligently hired co-employee are barred by the exclusivity rule. That rule applies to employer conduct except "intentional misconduct which goes beyond employer's failure to assure that the tools or substances used by the employee or the physical environment of a workplace are safe." (Cole v. Fair Oaks Fire Protection Dist. (1987) 43 Cal.3d 148, 158; Coit Drapery Cleaners, Inc. v. Sequoia Ins. Co. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 1595, 1606 ("any claim for mere negligence ' would be barred here by the workers' compensation laws ' [based on] negligent or improper supervision"). The exclusivity rule also bars Mr. Tindle from seeking recovery in tort from his employer for injuries sustained as a result of the physical attack committed against him by a co-employee during the scope and course of Mr. Tindle's employment. (Fretland v. County of Humboldt (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 1478, 1487). Mr. Tindle's allegation that UPS was or should have been aware of the co-employee's anger issues but nevertheless hired him, does not show UPS's "voluntary election" to adopt the assault by the co-employee as UPS's own conduct. Mr. Tindle's IIED claimed is also barred because it is not supported by facts showing intentional conduct going beyond the inherent risks of employment. (Cole (IIED claim based on reckless but not intentional conduct by the employer is precluded by the exclusivity rule)). If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Tindle is given leave to allege facts showing UPS ratified the intentionally tortious conduct of his co-employee and to allege intentional conduct by UPS beyond the normal risks of employment. Order signed. Judge; Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Not reported. =302/hk
